review:
Ska has done some extremely solid games in the past that I truly love playing and enjoy there dark, gritty, and somewhat unique style when approaching combat and how they want to get something across to you. This game was a clear attempt at something that didn't work AT ALL and was more ambitious than it should have been. It is also clear that they ran out of time in the process and settled on some things that only made this worse. This not even counting that this had an exclusivity deal with Epic games for a year and this alone regardless of intention makes this game an automatic failure for me. I read many of the reviews and found it hard to believe these guys strayed so far off what they usually do and into something that was completely unfun to play and I didn't like hunting either. This game may be somewhat better in co op but I wouldn't know as the online is dead. Many many issues with this game but can tell you the majority of them are about cheap tricks or things that just make the game not enjoyable. 

1. Poise, juggling, and falling. The entire time I played whether on my first character in heavy armor and high poise or my 2nd character a mage I got constantly interrupted and juggled into the air and often to death with no way out. Even smaller enemies can toss you in the air willy nilly and other enemies will jump in and perfectly knock you off ledges and cliffs at just the right angle. At 1 point near the end of the game I was smacked off a ledge and in trying to grapple back onto it, a new enemy who was summoned immediately jumped off the ledge and smacked me further down preventing me from climbing back up and on another chance of surviving jumped to another ledge just to have that same enemy drop 2 floors down onto the ledge I was trying to grab on to and guard it with his body so I can't then proceeded to slam dunk me down another 4 floors to my death. I cannot stress enough how often I died to things like this. Getting shot in the air by projectiles at the perfect time and angle just to stop me dead and I fall to my death. I am beyond sick of this.

2. You have to know before you know. Respeccing in this game was an afterthought as opposed to the original so trying out new builds just doesn't work without you sacrificing points to something you don't need. You need to know ahead of time what weapon or weapons you want to spec into and stick with it. Some weapons plain suck and you won't find out till it is too late. Magic isn't reliant on stats or spells you accumulate and instead are completely reliant on the weapon. Turns out all staves in the game scale with an E rating except 1 of them which you can only get at the end of the game. Seriously wtf?

3. All of the bosses suck. The vast majority of the game your chasing down different mages and mancers who specialize in particular kinds of magic. All of the mages you fight are chase bosses. You will chase them up and down and all around because they run non stop until you do enough damage to them to finally stop and face off. They tried to make this kind of like monster hunter for some reason and im still not sure why. Two separate times I ran into invincible ones that simply couldn't die. Here are a few of the different kinds of mages you will fight. You know Pyromancer, Cryomancer, and one of my favorites Mechanomancer. Game has to do with magic and all that and this 20 foot tall robot shooting pipe bombs and rockets shows up blowing me up through blocks and a poison thrower. Don't even get me started on the Umbramancer or lord help you the Bibliomancer as there isn't balance in the game. The game has a bunch of regular bosses throughout but they are so stupidly normal or easy im not even sure why they are there. Seriously how the hell can I go from a Pyromancer summoning demons and imps to a tall horse on 2 legs dressed like robin hood from the disney cartoon? Don't believe me? Go and fight Robin Horse and see what I mean.

No reason for me to go any further about this so just know that they have way better games out there and highly recommend you give those ones a try instead. And if for any reason you started on this game first before any of there others don't let this game be a reflection on what they can actually do.

等打到长老矮林进了屋子之后，到处是要砍两刀的火球怪、能硬控玩家的飞行椅子怪，要砍四刀才死的高攻兵，外加上随机刷新在地图里招小怪的法师，真的绝了，高周目无任何新内容，本来可以快速推掉的，结果非得挨个砍死路上的怪才能过去，跑酷不了一点，一跑随时被远程吸回椅子围殴，打吐了。

总之，只适合稍微体验下，多周目玩得非常无聊且难受……

Was hoping for a souls or metroidvania like, similar to the first game hopefully. Not all all. It's more like a crappy reimagining of monster hunter... that messes up with several core things that make monster hunter good. I love me some monster hunter, this... this is just ♥♥♥♥.

Now, maybe you get into some ok world exploration and lore eventually, but at least the starting area didn't feel good, and once you encounter your mages, hunt targets, things just start going badly. You go from exploring and pushing into new areas... to backtracking, fighting the same boss briefly some five or six times, then actually fighting the boss proper. Each of these fights is chaotic with multiple summons and respawning enemies in the area making it a mess of a fight, so not really fun. Finally get to the final location, it's an actual boss fight! You fight him and,... maybe win.  Maybe not... doesn't matter, you'll be fighting him again several times anyway. Literally, you'll find the same boss floating around and decide if you want to chase after it, or find a marker and pick it as your target again. Each time doing the slow chasing down across the map before finally fighting it. Overall, very annoying, and not really fun to keep doing.
So the core idea of this game, wasn't enjoyable, ok... was the game itself alright at least? Not really no...

Broken collisions, some enemies can block your movement, yet you cant hit them from pushing right up against them. Some enemy attacks have incroguent hitboxes that will hit you from like meters furhter than any visual indicates. Some enemies can attack you from other rooms, you are in one room, they are in the one next to you, before you go through that door and anything on the other side is visible to you, they can slam you with big long range melee or some ranged hits through it. 
Core combat just feels kinda ♥♥♥♥♥♥, most enemies are very much constant attack spammy with no stamina or clear gaps. While you very much are limitd by those. Most do have a gap so in one on one fights you can control distance or manuever to hit in an opening with the odd hit here or there. But enemies are often in groups and don't care about zoning. Many can literally jump from any part of a room to another, like you're two levels above them, they'll just "leap" up and through a wall and two floors to get to you. And at least with early stamina limits, good luck avoiding three guys doing nonstop moves that each only have a tiny gap every couple hits to get a counter move in...

And then awefull bosses like one that can summon minions, including one that makes the boss untouchable as long as it's alive. Guess how long it takes him to summon that minion again after it dies? Often not even five seconds, and no, that is not enough time to do a lot of damage. And with his moves and that minion floating around the screen if you want to avoid damage, it can literally be minutes before you can kill that ♥♥♥♥ sometimes. Just to do it again, twenty times to kill the boss... Not fun in the least bit. And more than a few moves do seem not 100% avoidable, even with only one enemy present some just drop random AOE across the entire map, if they randomly fall in the wrong locations in the wrong order, you cant roll out of one without getting hit by the next.
And that minion that sihelds him? It's floating... so you need to jump, he has attacks that ifyoure airborne and made ana ttack (cancles all horizontal movement and locks you there for a bit) he can use and you just can't dodge or avoid, so doing an aerial attack can let him for sure hurt you from across the map if he just finished his last move. Sure would be nice if he had moves that didn't have super long reach so I always had to avoid them... but nope, only one that meats that criteria he wont use unless I'm in range of it. Maybe I HAVE to have multiple allies to help for this boss, but nothing says that, and I play my games solo generally. And frankly, after getting him down to 15% health before getting locked out of any damage by his moves and that hsielding bugger for five minutes before dying... ya no F this. The two bosses I've so far fought, kinda ♥♥♥♥♥♥. Core gameplay, feels kinda ♥♥♥♥♥♥ (even worse than the first game, wasn't hte biggest fan of how it felt but still beet it proper as it had some good parts at least). Exploration and world, not doing great. Core gameplay loop seems to just be repetitive do this same stuff over and over... not enjoying, actually quitting this game early, something I almost never do. Might come play again in time... But for now, just don't find it fun at all after pushing an hour and a half into it. Worth saying, most of the game was very easy, like not fun but not hard, just annoying wait for a gap then hit, but without the nice dance or flow or special moves that make that more interesting. First death was a lancer that could charge and two shot me, and has bad collisions on his charge (hits you about three feet before the visual does, dodge early he'll nearly one shot you from full health). Second was falling to death when the grapple failed to lock on to a target right in front of me... And then the rest all came from that afformentioned boss that has a mob that shields him while covering the map with random explosions and stuff. First fight of it, easy enough, that second one with the shield guy, complete BS feeling.
Never really felt engaged during any of that.

EDIT: And pushed through, played some more. It got a little better in story, bit less repetitive on bosses. But overall still not worth playing. Regular enemies with grapple attacks they can spam that you can't poke or intercept, only roll to avoid, but if there's multiple that's not a reliable option, and they can even grapple and stab you out of another grapple and stab, so if more than one is present, you might die with no counter or inputs accepted. And theres more than one in a few areas.
A boss that's just horrible. Literally the worst in the past ten metroidvanias and souls likes I've played, worst I can actually recall playing (have played many more but memory gets fuzzy going back to far).
Camera force zooms out so you can barely see things, your health bar block one of the safe areas to attack the boss week spot. It's a platformer you'll get hurt if you fall on during this fight. The grapple and attack both keep getting ignored resulting in you falling to your death nearly half the time you jump. And this is after makign sure I had stamina, and wasn't/isn't being actively hit, as if you take a hit it will stop you from grappling/attacking for a bit. If you kill one weekspot early, you just sit and wait for awhile with nothing to do, this fight is just amazingly bad, not fun, completely unfair, like 95% rng. I dealt with 80% of the bosses health without takign one hit or point of damag one run. Then after that every jump or grapple near the boss failed and I'd just fall down, kept happening till there were 4 enemies up and I got spammed to death from falls and lasers and projectiles. Not like I was playing differently, just the insane broken mechanics/inputs/wahtever of this fight (yes input logger up confirmed my inputs on my controller were working, it was the game, also not doing this nearly that often outside of that boss room, though it does drop inputs or fail on grapples sometimes anywhere. Game is more than a bit finicky...)

Well, that fight, the worst boss I can recall that makes me question if the devs every play tested it, was enough for me to uninstall. First game I've done that with in over a year or so that wasn't for technical reasons that kept me from actually playing/completing it properly. So if this game got me to quit, take that for what it's worth. It's really not worth playing, and very frustrating and annoying once you are at many points.

Enemies camp ledges and attack with little to no cooldown, including bosses and mages. They are incredibly easy to stagger and generally vulnerable to rolling towards them and attacking from behind. Combat was never a major selling point for me in Sanctuary, but in Sacrifice, it is notably worse. 

With The Tireless Exalted, for example, they have four attacks — two slow staff slams, a fast censer slam, and a spirit-summoning animation (these spirits build up a status that can almost one-shot you when it procs). The first two attacks give the impression of being easy to punish, but the boss can just use their fast attack on a whim, encouraging you to stay at the edges of the arena to bait out their summoning attack where they're extremely vulnerable. Things change in P2 when one of their slams also summons spirit projectiles, but these otherwise lethal projectiles can be consistently avoided if you simply tank the initial slam and abuse your stagger I-frames afterward. The result is a counterintuitive boss that simply isn't fun to fight against.

Not all bosses and combat encounters are like this, but on the whole, the highest point of combat was simply inoffensiveness, with the lowest being frustration. There are also many other tangentially-related annoyances like: roaming mages spawning in small spaces and camping ledges, effectively blocking progression until they warp away; multiple mages (even the same two mages on rare occasion, and as many as three separate mages) fighting in the same area, wherein your best approach for survival is to simply *disengage* from combat entirely until it's over; hazeburnt enemies just being a general nuisance during mage hunts; mage hunts being more dangerous than the actual mages themselves.

That said, I did admittedly finish the game, and not begrudgingly. There was enough of the DNA of Sanctuary to make me stick around, as I did truly enjoy that game and its aesthetic. The hub world is cozy enough, traversal is more fun, and the weapon system is a change of pace (I'm personally indifferent to it). The first few starting areas were a bit lackluster, but the last two were actually interesting to explore and a nice change of scenery. If the combat was reworked to feel more engaging and less cheap (as well as making roaming mages more rare), I think I'd enjoy this game a lot more, but I can only recommend it if it's on sale, you enjoyed Sanctuary, and are willing to give it a chance.

和好友Jylx联机打完了成就。

盐1在我心目中，是仅次于Hollow Knight的存在。盐2作出了很多大胆的尝试，最显著的是，它实际上是个联机游戏，类型中也是把“在线合作”放在第一位。联机导致游戏难度大大降低，两人一左一右开弓，能对任何怪无限削韧，打大部分boss也是信手拈来。不过怪物都是人均疯狗，明显看出不是单机玩家能够应付的。

很多怪还会击飞玩家。例如好友被击飞到墙下后，费了半天劲爬上来，结果又被击飞下去；或者两人站在悬崖边，忽然被霸王龙从后边双双顶飞下去（这霸王龙的肤色跟背景接近，所以很多时候很难看清它）；或者跑图时，一个不注意被陷阱击飞到遥远的远方...大概因为这些缘故，本作的坠落伤害非常低。

整个联机过程可以说非常欢乐，当然，大量重复的狩猎，也让人有些疲劳。而二代在美术风格和箱庭设计上，都完全不逊于一代，很多小怪的设计也很有感觉。但“狩猎”明显是个失败的设计，除却它对单机玩家不友好外，狩猎完的报酬也不尽人意，个人认为起码应该掉精良的武器和装备，这才足以让玩家获得狩猎成功后的成就感。

二代在其他不少地方也做了尝试，但个人认为同样都是较为失败的，这里就不一一阐述。如果有制作组想做类银河战士恶魔城类型游戏，那么完全可以把二代当做反面教材（很遗憾它落到了这种地步）。

注意：如果想一个周目全成就，需要1--在结局前备份存档；2--两个玩家都需在在结局前备份存档，否则其中一个玩家不会跳成就。
还不错，值得一玩，但是小怪设定太强，多周目可玩性较低，整体不如一代。

I don't understand why people hate on this game so much, I mean I do, people like Salt and Sanctuary more and for some reasons beyond my comprehension that's the reason why this game sucks so much.

Let me get one thing straight, this isn't Salt and Sanctuary, it doesn't need to be, we should compare the games to each other sure they're sequels afterall, but not on this level, the game is good, even if you like Sanctuary more.

Salt and Sacrifice isn't just a "2D Dark Souls" like many people likes to claim, it borrows elements from Monster Hunter, and they fit right in a souls like game, since bosses having a weakness to specific elements is a trope in this genre, this fact alone made me appreciate the game even more, is always good to see creativity in this genre.

The things that made Sanctuary good are still here, the combat is meaty and fun, not as violent doe, I really liked the detail in the first game were blood would splatter on the walls and your weapon would be coated in blood after a counter attack, but this game still has strong tone and atmosphere, with a bigger and broader collor pallet, proving that you dont need to stick with black and white to make a dark fantasy setting work.

The graphics are a improvement from the first game, the drawings are more comprehensive and detailed, it no longer feels like the sketches of a edgy teenager (eventhough that is part of Ska Studio's apeal), but i feels like that teenager took some art classes and improved, the character design is still unique and creative, even if they're a little generic compared to Sanctuary.

I like the use of the black and white distortion when you devour a Mage, start a hunt or perform a counter it's used often enough to feel rewarding.

The new class of weapons, mainly the Vanguard weapons, are a huge welcome, I like the combos they have and the animations, the fact that each spell is tied to a weapon helps to streamline the game and make it less confusing.

The story is good, confusing and hard to understand? Yes, but that always been the case, in fact, I like the story of Sacrifice more than Sanctuary, because the stakes are more personal, when I got to Heart of Altarstone, defeated the last boss there, and use the Truth Elixir, it felt like one of the most rewarding reveals in a video game ever.

The game isn't perfect, one of the main attractions are the Mage hunts, but they do end up feeling repetitive towards the end, mainly because you do the same thing to hunt all of them and there's no real investigation going on, you just run around the map until they decide that's their time to die, it would be cool if each of them had a actual lair, like the bosses in Sanctuary, also just like the Guardians in this game, I would say that the fights with the Guardians are more unique than the ones with the mages.

But the Mage hunts proves to me how good the level design is, some areas feels baren because they're meant to be the little arenas you fight the mages in, but going around the map is organic and gets pretty chaotic, as the mobs summoned by the Mage fights the local mobs and each Mage fights each other fits right in with the storys narrative, these Mages are so powerful that they bend reality and what were doing is not apparently good or bad, as were doing a lot of damage just like the Mages by just hunting them.

One thing this game does better than Sanctuary is the use of the Tools compared to Brands, the Tools are fun to use and used often, and one of them is even used in combat, the star of the show, the grapple hook, it would be nice if we could use it on tall mobs rather than just Mages.

And because of the Monster Hunter inspiration, having to change strategies or load outs to defeat a specific Guardian or Mage never felt so rewarding, the difference you can have by just switching your weapon is huge, and you can also craft items tied to each mage, making each strategy even more reliable and fun to play.

Another improvement is that heavy armor isn't a huge hurdle like the first game, for starters, the debuff you get for having a carry weight close to your limit isn't as bad, and this game changed how the skill tree works so farming isn't as bad, the new system is so good they even changed Sanctuary's skill tree in the enchanted edition.

This game is a little easier than Sanctuary, well, at least I think, I played Sanctuary a lot and I'm pretty familiar with Ska studios games, but this and how they changed the skill tree only makes the game balanced, in the sense that any strategy is valid, every time I go online I always see someone wearing a completely different loadout.

The multi-player in this game is also a huge upgrade, it isn't as active like it was and being a invader is much harder that a cooperator, but it's so much fun, the game gets even more chaotic and so much strategy options reveal themselves.

Once there was 3 mages on the same arena, I was so much fun to see them fighting each other and for me to some how defeat all 3 of them, and a other time when I was a cooperator, a lot of people started to join in and it was pure chaos, 2 invaders showed up followed by each one of their opposite factions, everyone was scrambling figuring our what to do.

I love this game, that's it, and this is coming from someone that played a lot of Sanctuary too, I can't really decide on with one I like better because both of them are so different, but this game can carry itself on he's own well enough.

The levels are not optimised for the various mages, the DPS is way too high, being able to be air-juggled to death is not fun and heavy armour feels like a worthless investment. Also, no proper respeccing, respec one at a time for a limited number; too bad if you specced into heavy armor or a bad weapon :)
Oh, and good luck finding nearly anything, you need to hunt everything to death and then some.

If the difficulty was toned down to less then "die in two hits to a combo you cannot break out of" it's be fine, but right now? Nah, avoid this like the plague. Go play Salt and Sanctuary, that was fun (unless the backported some of these terrible machanics...)

Do you know how gutted I was to finally get my hands on Salt and Sacrifice, only to discover I don't like it?
I don't know what it is. It feels like what I enjoyed about the first game was ground down like the rough edges of a project. It's techncially more refined, technically better balanced - but it feels like it lacks... *something* I liked more.
Why do so many weapons feel so bad? The magic system I loved in one is gone, replaced with... something very different, and something that just feels *weak* and bad. It feels like it's entirely balanced around the PvP, so all the spells can't be anything too crazy or too strong, but now I feel like I'm throwing magic throwing knives at a boss for no damage.
Why exactly is a Vanguard weapon (sword and shields, scale mostly with strength), have a random wind weapon in it that scales with dex? You can't even make proper use of that without investing heavily into a stat that doesn't benefit the Vanguards at all.

The bosses all feel... Weak? Some are just annoying, the "Pulling instant attacks out of their ass" kind, but they're never that hard? None of the main-line bosses are. The monster-hunter like mages go from being easy to "♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, what were they thinking?" Yeah, the vampire mage is an awesome idea. Shame the fight boils down to him spamming endless combos that send it across the small arena you're relegated to and it stunlocks and juggles insanely hard.
Shame the huge mech boss made of wood that looks AWESOME is "I just have to expect that at any moment it can pull out its strongest attack with 0 wind up that does 80% of my HP and it combos into and out of anything."
It's a lot of that in the end game.

I really, really wanted to enjoy this game. I don't even mind it's a weird monster hunter dark souls like, but I don't really feel like it plays to the strengths of monster hunter, or even really dark souls. For all of the weirdness and quirks I loved about the first game, some of that charm is gone in this one. The music is still good most of the time, sometimes it feels a bit... I don't want to say bad? I don't think it is, but it just seems odd.
Some of the atmospheric tracks are wonderful. Bol Gerahn has one of my favourite tracks.
I think a lot of the story is interesting, I love how grim a lot of this game can feel. I really enjoy the setting in general, I enjoy some of the lore implications I've found in this game and how it ties into the world of Salt and (x).

Hell, I love some of the changes actually. Ranged weapons being more limited, but actually being useful is nice? Something any build can have? Axes for strength, knives or bows for dex, staves that use some kind of powder as a catalyst for an arcane reaction for... arcane builds. It's cool, I like it.
But then you have things like the bombs. So many of them are just. Weak and bad, to be honest. Why do we have holy water jars that scale with convinction, when Bombs give you strength for unlocking, and then it does *bad* damage and has a terrible scaling? What about the jars of water that inflict the feeling of drowning in the deep sea just... being ♥♥♥♥? i feel like there's a lot of that in this game.
I don't like some of the areas. Some enemies are just annoying to fight. Some areas just feel awful, like the area added in Traitor's Tomb. Some of the areas I just, can't get down. I cannot navigate them well for the life of me, I CANNOT commit them to memory and I do not know why.

There's more I want to say about this game, but I just don't know how.
I don't think it's bad to experiment. It's good to, actually. Stagnation is how you get games that feel samey and boring and repetitive. Experimentation might not always hit the mark, but you'll still learn something along the way, and that might inform the direction of your next project. 
I wish I liked where this experiment landed. But I did not.

NOT Salt and Sanctuary 2, although seemingly connected one way or another to the first game however not directly.

If you want a gameplay loop similar to Salt 1, then this game is NOT for you. 

Salt and Sacrifice's gameplay loop sees you explore, get this game's 'brand' equivalent, hunt all mages in said area, defeat an Area boss (separate from mages) and then move to the next area and repeat. Unlike Salt 1, the game more-or-less forces your hand into a magic-specced build type if you want full efficiency out of your weapons (or variety) as a good chunk (not counting the 'tier 1' starter / normal damage type weapons) scale with one of the two magic stats (basically INT or FAITH). You can stick with your normal weapon as they have decent scaling from what I can tell and there are a few other neutral damage type weapons you can find but for the most part if you want a new weapon you're going to be crafting one from the materials you obtain from slain mages, which incidentally require you to spec into magic to access their full moveset (basically Weapon Arts). 

Despite all this I find myself enjoying the game, I'm not really sure what it is about it that's fun though. If you want to try it buy it on sale or watch a video to get a grasp of the gameplay loop better, alternatively you can try playing it for under 2 hours and see if you like it though i'm not 100% the gameplay loop is covered in that time, only about 65%.

Niedliche Grafik, unübersichtliche Darstellung und grauenhafte Kämpfe.

Unsere Aufgabe ist es, Magier zu töten. Das machen wir mit einem selbsterstellten Charakter und reichlich Frustresistenz. Die Grafik ist dabei recht hübsch anzuschauen. Der Charakter ist nett gezeichnet, leider etwas hakelig animiert, die Hintergründe recht stimmungsvoll. Manchmal gibt es Fragezeichen, wenn sich z. B. der Mond mit meiner Blickrichtung mitbewegt. Eine Kleinigkeit, stört aber dennoch etwas. Sound und Musik sind passend. Die Texte können auf Deutsch eingestellt werden, verwundern aber manchmal durch die Übersetzung (ich sage nur Schnurrhaare). Leider scheitert das Spiel am zentralen Gameplay-Element: den Kämpfen. Mein Charakter kommt mir unglaublich träge und behäbig vor, die Steuerung reagiert schwammig und teils ungenau. Die Bossgegner bleiben gerne auf Distanz, was für Nahkämpfer eher so mittel ist. Auch die Grafik kommt da schon mal als Gegner daher, wenn der eigene Charakter weggeschlagen wird und man erst einige Zeit benötigt, um ihn wiederzuentdecken. Dann wartet die Umgebung noch dazu mit recht fiesen Fallen auf, die kaum zu entdecken sind. Auch die einfachen "Fußsoldaten" sind alleine Kanonenfutter, aber schon zu zweit können die einen in die Zange nehmen, aus der man nicht mehr herauskommt. Und was soll eigentlich der Quatsch, dass ich den Bossgegner erst jagen muss? Da laufe ich kreuz und quer durch das Level dem Magier hinterher, kämpfe mit herbeigerufenen Kreaturen und sehe, wie er verschwindet. Also wieder hinterherlaufen, kämpfen usw. Beim ersten Teil hatte ich noch Spaß, der ging mir hier komplett abhanden.

Sat and Sacrifice - це продовження колись популярного 2д соулс лайка Salt and Sanctuary з елементами метроїдванії. Із нового в цій грі - це так звана охота на магів. Максимально душна механіка, що заставляє вас бігати за однотипними мінібосами по усій локації. Скіпнути її не вийде, бо по перше купа дверей на карті зачинена і відкрити їх можна тільки вбивши певну кількість цих магів. А по друге з цих мінібосів та їх міньйонів падають дуже потрібні реси для крафту нової броні, зброї та іншого. Мені взагалі здасться що разраби  після того, як побачили що їхня гра проходиться годин так за 10, вирішили що це мало та розтягнули час проходження у 2,5 рази додавши цю залупу. Також у грі немає карти і це ну дуже бісить. З кожним  разом бігаючи вже десятий круг по локації туди-сюди у пошуках потрібної двері або нового проходу (метроїдванія жеж) так і хочеться послати разрабів та дропнути гру. Ця гра настільки хоче бути схожою на Темні Душі, що в неї завезли купу онлайн механік, але наразі толку від них нуль бо немає з ким грати. Босів мало і усі вони не цікаві та дуже примітивні. В цілому гра вийшла дуже посереднія, з  купою душних або взагалі  непотрібних механік. Краще взяти або навіть пепепройти першу частину. Купувати можна, але з дуже великою знижкою і тільки якщо ви пройшли усі інші хороші солси.

While I haven't finished this game yet, there's a lot more to this game that I can't really get into, so I'll just quickly cover what this game does, what I like, what I dislike, and some oddities I found with this game.

What I like:
The Artstyle: This game is a massive improvement on the first game's graphics, with vastly better animations, and the overall art used in this game has been cleaned up.
The Tools: Like in the first game, you get upgrades that allow you to explore more parts of the map as you get to certain points in the game. In this game, you start out with the Wall Jump ability (previously an unlockable ability), and get items like a Grappling Hook to swing across parts of the level, a gem to activate magic circles to create new paths, and a device to activate Ziplines, which allows the level design to be significantly different from the first game.
The NPCs: NPCs are very simple in this game: After finding and speaking to one, they'll return to the main hub of the game known as Pardoner's Veil. It makes it easier to return to them to buy things from them or just talk with them. Not every NPC returns there, but at least you can find most here.
Regaining Lost Salt: Instead of having to kill a stronger version of the enemy that killed you prior, or killing a stray Salt Bat you might even die at (since in most cases, you likely died from things like falls), in this game, it's more like how Dark Souls does it, with a spot on the ground where you can simply run up to and quickly grab. Much easier, much faster.
No More Covenants (technically): In the first game, one of the more annoying aspects of the game, while original, was the covenants. Each covenant has it's perks, with unique rewards, and items for sale, but because you can only be a part of one covenant at a time, getting items from other covenants was always annoying, since you had to either skip getting the item you want from the covenant, or lose progress for the covenant you were part of. Not to mention, even the healing items were different, with some being straight up worse than the others. All this to say, it's far more simplified in this game. When you find someone who is the leader of the covenant, you automatically get the items associated with the covenant, and can purchase items from the singular representative of the covenant: No more having to worry about if you have enough Stone Merchants or Stone Leaders, or who you're a part of. If you want the armor from one guy and one weapon from another, you absolutely can.
Multiplayer: While I haven't played the multiplayer in this game yet, it's been vastly improved in comparison to the first game. In the first game, it's exclusively shared screen multiplayer, which means the only way to play the game online with a friend is through Steam Remote Play. This game has proper matchmaking features, similar to how Dark Souls does it, with the ability to raid and aid other players. Also if you dislike PvP, don't worry: Even with online play turned on, nobody's online, so you're highly unlikely to have somebody invade. I'm not sure if the game has seemless Co-Op like how the first game did, but it does have a password system for connecting with a friend, so I'm curious how that will work.
Sprinting: You can sprint in this game. With the push of a button, at the start of the game, you can sprint. And you don't have to worry about Stamina usage unless enemies are around. It's really that simple.

What I dislike:
Currency: In the first game, there are two currencies: Gold and Salt. Salt levels you up and Gold is spent on items at your covenants. This game is somewhat like that, with two currencies, Silver (which is otherwise identical to Gold), and Salt, which is still used to level up, but because of how this game is structured, it feels different. First off, many items in the game have the same price, which means they all feel as worthless as each item, and you won't end up buying much more other than Salt Pieces. Not to mention, very few NPCs actually accept Silver (which I'll explain in a moment), and the enemies give such little Salt, the only way to reliably level up is by killing Mages. Yes, killing the main bosses of the game is the only way to reliably and semi quickly level up, which you will need to do.
Repetitive: A majority of this game is fighting the same few Mages over and over again. There are two kinds of Mages: Named and Nameless Mages. Named Mages are needed to progress the game, with 5 being on each stage, and there's usually 10 Nameless Mages on each stage, making 15 Mages you can hunt each stage. The 5 Named Mages open up doors to more areas, the Nameless Mages exist mainly to just get Salt and resources to build weapons and armor. 5 of the Nameless Mages you find in exactly the same way as you do finding Named Mages, meaning sometimes you find progress, sometimes you waste your time, and the other 5 Nameless Mages are found from a book, which just gives you their hunts immediately that you can select from the portal. None of these fights have any difference to where you fight them, barely any variation to who you fight, and no difference in what you get for beating them. This isn't even getting into the random Nameless Mages that just wonder around aimlessly that slow you down while exploring.
The Mage Fights: The Mage fights are very frustrating if you attempt to use any combination of heavy weapons and/or armor. If you use heavy armor or slow weapons, you'll be completely unable to combat them easily, since they attack faster the lower their health gets, and you can't meaningfully tank attacks during their fights. Also, remember those roaming Nameless Mages? Sometimes, the Mage you're hunting stops by one of these roaming Mages and decided to have a dual with him. Now you have to decide to either save your resources and let them duke it out, or save your time and target the Mage you're hunting to get him to leave. Another case of roaming Mages being annoying.
The Covenants: While it is more streamlined from Sanctuary, it still has problems. In order to buy the weapons and items from the covenants, you need to get their covenant specific tokens. How do you get these? By playing the abandoned multiplayer. You're better off either cheating or using mods to get these items (R.I.P. console players).
Heavy Armor: It's not strong enough to tank damage, doesn't have enough poise to keep your attacks from being interrupted, and makes you too slow to dodge. Unless you're using it for drip, just use light armor.

What I Find Odd:
The Levels: While there is more to explore in this game compared to the first one, it's presented in the form of levels. As in, stages you play individually. They're not connected at all, except through the same portal. If you wanna explore level 1, but you need something from level 3, you need to leave level 1, beat level 2, find the thing in level 3, then go back to level 1 so you can continue exploring. I don't know if I'm explaining it well, but it'll make more sense if you play the game or watch gameplay.
Weapon Categories: It was very simple in Sanctuary: Great Hammers are leveled with the Great Hammer skill, Swords are leveled with the Sword skill, and Crossbows are leveled with the Crossbow skill. In this game, it gets confusing: Greataxes are in the Greathammer skill category, Swords have three different categories with Vangards (which is the only way to use shields), Highblades, and Rapiers, and there's three different kinds of thrown weapons (thrown axes, thrown knives, and throwables). This isn't even getting into the 5 different skills that weapons scale with.

While I do enjoy the game, it feels like it's longer than Sanctuary without having more. I have been using the same armor and barely been upgrading it for the past 20hrs, and I haven't felt any significant change in damage taken yet. Maybe just pick this game up during a sale and you should be fine.

First off, we all must realize this is not Salt and Sanctuary 2, it's Salt and Sacrifice, so I'll judge the game on it's own terms, so...

 [h2] Souls-like meets Monster Hunter [/h2] 
Picture this scenario: you're on a hunt in a MH game and the monster flees from you, so you follow. You marked it with a paintball, or are tracking with your Scoutflies or checking it's position on the map via Cohoot, whatever. 
Suddenly you turn a corner and a randomly respawning Bullfango charges at you and takes a solid 1/4th of your HP bar. Not only that, you're pushed into a Slagtoth who for some reason also finds your presence offensive and belly-flops another half of your remaining HP away. 
You're almost dead but managed to survive. Annoyed you waste a mega potion to recover and continue with the hunt but WHAM! tripped on a nigh-invisible tripwire and get bushwhacked by some trap which ragdolls you off a cliff. 
Again you lucked out and survived, still you're really pissed now, down a couple mega potions and actually manage to track down your target, along the way dodging attacks from Kelbis and Popos who against all logic are aggroed too, but oh no! what's this? Here comes another monster which btw can spawn little minions of it's own and now he wants a piece of both your ass and your target's, too! 
And the monster you actually wanted to hunt? Yeah, it can also spawn minions, which he does right before he pisses off again to another part of the map.

Does this sound fun to you? 

If not, well now you know why blending genres willy-nilly is not that hot of an idea. 

The thing is this game is not that bad, after all I 100% it, but damn if it doesn't suffer from situations like the ones I described. You cannot have a game where the aim is hunting down big, dangerous and resilient baddies who move all across the arena when both the environment you give chase AND it's denizens are as deadly if not even more so than said targets.

Which brings me to the level design itself. The maps just don't feel as well designed as Salt and Sanctuary's since they have little to no signposting to differentiate between areas, making it difficult to familiarize yourself with them and thus being able to recognize the paths you should take, instead you have a "mage sense" which always points you to the general location of the boss you're hunting. 

To me they felt as jumbled assortments of platforms with a few designated "fighting arenas" for the mages to stop and fight you, but there was nothing organic about many of them. A throne room here or a big vault there, but on the whole it felt you were just fighting on the street corner or random cave you happen to stumble on the boss.

And on the subject of maps, just include an *actual* map already! A souls-like this might be, but it borrows heavily from the Metroidvania genre, and you know what actually makes those games fun? Having something to help you figure out your position, unexplored areas, yet-unopened doors, etc. i.e. a map.

Like I said it's not all bad, the weapons are pretty fun and the mages are, within the self-imposed limitations of their design, pretty varied and also entertaining to fight. I mention those limitations since they all fit the design category of "big dude". No quick and nimble fighters like the False Jester, no flying caster like The Witch of the Lake, no stationary-but-spammy-target like the Dried King, no interesting gank squads like the Forgotten King and co. There ARE a couple duo fights, but are non-mages and felt pretty underwhelming. 
Imagine if you had a Monster Hunter game where there are no Kirins, no Rathians, no Zinogres, no Najaralas or Gore Magalas. Instead all the monsters are variations of Tetsucabra, only this one uses fireballs, that one chains lighting on the floor, the one on the corner is just a really hard-scaled, physical type and yet another who can croak AND cloak! You can now start to imagine the design limitations of the mages here. 

Overall, I would only recommend the game to people who really dig souls-likes, and even then with big caveats.

Innovation through combination is all well and good, but don't just try to put bacon on ice cream, you'll just end up with a stomachache.

I'm not sure why people dislike this sequel so much compared to the first game. The difficulty is probably about the same. There are a handful of difficulty spikes that will feel strange and annoying, but overall feels better balanced than the first game. Mechanics feel slightly less jank. There is still metroid-vania-style exploration into dangerous areas and souls-like build crafting. You still find a handful of special armor and weapons if you explore, and you can back track to previous areas and find new areas. An NPC will give you rumors to help hint at some interesting stuff. Each area is quite large, spanning something  that feels on the order of two areas together in the first game. The graphics are smoother than the first game. One big complaint I see in other reviews is that people don't like the gameplay loop of chasing a mage around as it spawns minions - but as it moves about the level, you can rest and regain all your supplies without losing any progress. You can run away. Mobs and even mages will fight each other, so if you don't do something dumb you can mop up the mess with ease. Very frequently you can rest and resupply *right before* the health-bar fight, so sometimes there aren't even runbacks. I can only wonder what the multiplayer mechanics would have been like, since that scene appears to be dead. Overall, I've enjoyed the game so far. If you like the first game, I think you'll probably like this one if you keep an open mind. I'll update this review if my attitude changes by the time I finish it.

i like this game, but i understand the disappointment with the overall game by some folks, because this is a sequel to the salt and sanctuary game.

its practically worth understanding this is is very different game compare to the sanctuary, the whole gameplay is very different. where in the original was very much your metroidvania game that gives out route to different bosses and abilities to access new areas in a different order. this game isn't that as it is more in line with something similar to monster hunter. there is bosses for sure, but its not as interesting as the bosses in the sanctuary, the mages are cool albeit repetitive.

the mages falls into a similar blueprint as in they have a similar design to each other whether it be minions or moves. most mages usually consist of 3 type of minions which is small, mid, and big. this is the same for most of the mages which could result in a repetitive gameplay where you need to deal the mobs first then the mages. 

the mages moveset could be describe as limited, as many of them usually have some attack in a form aoe, or anything that restrict your movement which result in you needing to block or dodge consistently. they're attack is a bit fast or spammy felt, which is a shame i like the design of the mages. they're cool and interesting, its just the fight left a lot to desire. there is also the issue with mages cant be killed if they are not in their areas, but the boss can be stuck in between which result in them having a higher health than usual. 

It's practically weird cause the tutorial boss, the one you die to. Has a better moveset because all of them are telegraph with enough time for you to react, and doesn't require you to block. I don't know what happen to this game to have such a large amount of bosses but doesn't have much enjoyment compare to the tutorial. So whoever design that goat boss is the goat and the devs should practically follow that philosophy of boss design. 

the whole gameplay cannot be approach in a souls like fashion where you dodge everything, but rather blocking enemies attack combine doing the ranged attack that you can do when the boss do their aoes. its pretty different combat compare to sanctuary, so if you are expecting the same combat then unfortunately its a no.

one thing i did found it funny was the multiplayer aspect, as in pvp. very ambitious to have it. unfortunately its dead and you cant grind the tokens for the faction at a fair time cause the rate you get from dailies just take way too long. 50 token for a single equipment is a bit insane. 

the maps are great though, there is a lot backtracking and secrets so i didnt have any major gripes with it, though there are some frustration with traps being there as it result the chasing of mages becomes more annoying. 

The weapons are awesome, definitely the highlight for me. As most weapons got their runic arcs which can spice up the gameplay, though most playthrough you would play 1 or 2 weapon type. 

i would recommend this game under the expectation that coming to this game as its own thing rather as a sequel that is intended by the devs. That with also buying this game under steam sale

I really want to like this game. It has unique visual, interesting mage hunts, fantastic movement and combat. Yet, the just the few bad ideas completely kill this game for me.

For one, this game wanted to replicate the feel of Sekiro by making parry it's central combat mechanic. But the developers did not understand what made deflect so good in Sekiro. For one, parry in this game is very precise. Considering how much you have to do it, that's not a good thing. Any essential mechanic needs to be easy to perform (but hard to master). While you can technically complete most of the game without parrying once, you will have a REALLY bad time in end game locations, since at least few mages straight up force you to parry. 
The awkwardness of the parry is futher increased by the fact that nearly every enemy in this game either doesn't have a wind-up animation or has one that is way too fast to react to. In Sekiro, your Deflect interrupts your other actions, meaning that if an enemy pulls out some hard to react move, if you press the button right in time, you're good, even if you're in the middle of an animation.

These things are not bad by themselves. They would work perfectly in a game focused on 1-on-1 fights. However, this game is not about that. It is about hordes of enemies. Enemies always roam the levels in packs, mages almost always summon enemies in numbers. Your ability to execute parries perfectly suffers greatly when you have to do it against big hordes of enemies.

This game being 2D also suffers from this. It is often impossible to avoid damage, considering that some attacks require you to dodge behind the enemy, yet you simply cannot do that with such large amount of bodies in front of you. You can technically use parry to avoid those attacks, but like I mentioned above, the mechanic itself is unfun to do. Not to mention that you can only parry the first enemy in line, so it doesn't save you against hordes either.

And I'm not even going to talk about how this game is named in the same way as dev's previous game, dispite having nearly zero similarities. This dead horse has already been beaten by nearly everyone.

⚔️ Salt and Sacrifice – Brutal, oscuro y profundamente recompensante ⭐⭐⭐⭐☆ (8.5/10)

Salt and Sacrifice es un soulslike de acción y exploración 2D que toma lo mejor de su predecesor (Salt and Sanctuary) y lo expande con un mundo más abierto, enemigos más variados y una progresión más satisfactoria. Aquí, encarnas a un Cazador de Brujas (o Brujo) en un reino plagado de herejías, monstros grotescos y trampas mortales.

⚔️ El combate es tenso y técnico: cada arma tiene peso, cada esquiva cuenta y cada enemigo requiere atención. La variedad de builds posibles (fuerza, destreza, magia, híbridos) amplía enormemente las formas de abordar encuentros y jefes.

🌍 La exploración no es lineal: cada región tiene su propio ambiente, secretos bien escondidos y rutas alternativas que descubrir. El sentido de descubrimiento constante refuerza la rejugabilidad.

🏗️ El sistema de progresión se siente robusto: mejoras de equipo, elección de atributos y construcción de personajes que realmente cambian cómo juegas. Encajar bien tu armadura, armas y hechizos puede transformar un enfrentamiento imposible en algo manejable.

🎨 Visualmente mantiene un estilo oscuro y detallado con estética gótica y cuerpos desmembrados que refuerzan su tono sombrío. La música y efectos sonoros acompañan cada escena con peso.

⏱ Duración para completar al 100 % de logros: entre 30 y 40 horas, dependiendo de cuánto explores, domines jefes opcionales y pruebes distintas builds.

Lo mejor
✔️ Combate profundo y satisfactorio
✔️ Mundo diverso con exploración libre
✔️ Personalización de personajes muy amplia

A mejorar
➖ Dificultad elevada que puede frustrar al principio
➖ Ritmo irregular en zonas de backtracking
➖ Menos narrativa directa y más ensayo/error

Salt and Sacrifice es ideal si te apasionan los soulslike exigentes con un mundo que recompensa la curiosidad y la maestría. Una experiencia amarga, desafiante… y profundamente memorable.

No entiendo este juego, intenta ser 50 cosas a la vez y falla estrepitosamente en todas ellas. Es repetitivo, tedioso, torpe y por algún motivo se siente peor que en el Sanctuary. Vayamos por partes.

-Bosses: Creo que de las peores cosas, sino la peor. No entiendo la gracia de pelearte 20 veces con el mismo tío y aun después de eso, ir buscandolo por todo el mapa, siendo este vertical y vertiginoso. Horrible. Ya no hablemos de cuando se cuelan 2 magos a la vez. Buena suerte. 

-Enemigos: De nuevo, de lo peor. Hasta el minion mas pequeño es capaz de mandarte a Cancún de un macoco. Da igual la armadura y poise que tengas. Las peleas se sienten como un partido de tenis, donde tu eres la pelota y los mobs están jugando la final de Wimbledon. Genuinamente insoportable. 

-Mapas: No están mal, pero la primera entrega tenía mas personalidad. Demasiado vertical y engatusado. Se me hicieron repetitivos e incluso como querer volver a incorporar los de la primera entrega, pero peores. 

-Gameloop: Mata y farmea a los mismos tios, una y otra vez. Y cuando crees que es suficiente, vence a algun jefe de zona, consigue una habilidad y pasa a la siguiente. Honestamente, no sé a quién se le ocurrió esta idea. Pero la verdad que fue un fallo como una casa y el motivo por el que mucha gente ha tirado el juego. Se sabe que los soulslike/metroidvania están muy quemados. Pero hubiera preferido eso, en lugar a lo que sea el juego este sin sentido. 

En definitiva, lo pille de rebajas y aún así me llevé un chasco enorme. Y lo esperé con ganas cuando se anuncio ya que la precuela me flipó. Deberia haberse quedado en la Epic Games Store xD

Sentimientos muy encontrados con Salt and Sacrifice, y me duele tener que marcar esta reseña como negativa porque el juego no es malo. El mayor problema que tiene es que es el sucesor de Salt and Sanctuary, un juego por el que pagué 5 euros y habría pagado 60 tranquilamente.

El juego no es que sea malo, pero ha adpotado un estilo que creo que no le conviene para nada, en el primer juego el mapa y la exploración estaban muy bien logrados, y en este el juego es literalmente un Monster Hunter, que no está mal, pero si hubieran sacado literalmente un juego como el anterior con el mismo gameplay, alguna cosa más y un mapa nuevo, lo hubiera preferido 100 veces más.

El gameplay loop nuevo es "meh", cazar magos, los sigues un rato por el mapa y cuando los acorralas los matas para pillar materiales y hacer armas más fuertes para cazar más magos, no tiene mucho más, está ok.

Preferiría darle a esta reseña un voto neutro más que negativo, porque lo acabaré jugando más y seguramente me lo pase, pero es que Salt and Sanctuary capturó a una audiencia que esperaba con ansias más y mejor, y dicen que a veces la cosa es innovarse o morir... y tristemente Salt and Sacrifice innovó y se murió.

A very strong 2D Soulslike Metroidvania, that I praise for its beautiful hand-drawn art, deep combat, satisfying Mage Hunts, and engaging jolly co-op! 

Positive Aspects

Art & Atmosphere: Hand-drawn, dark, and desolate aesthetic with beautiful backgrounds and unique character designs that effectively convey its ethereal, yet haunting world.
Combat: Deep, fluid, and satisfying 2D combat with light/heavy attacks, dodges, and blocks, offering great customization with various weapons and armor.
Mage Hunts: Epic boss battles against powerful Mages provide satisfying, varied challenges that are central to the experience.
Exploration: Large, interconnected levels encourage backtracking with new traversal abilities (grappling, updrafts) and hide meaningful rewards.
Co-op: An easy-to-initiate online co-op system adds to the fun.
Progression: Deep RPG systems with extensive skill trees and crafting allow for diverse playstyles. 

Criticisms & Drawbacks

Familiarity: Doesn't innovate much, feeling very similar to its predecessor, Salt and Sanctuary, which might feel stale to some.
Traversal: Can be stiff or clunky, requiring pinpoint accuracy at times, making movement less satisfying.
Repetition: Common enemies can feel samey, and the gameplay loop, while addictive, can become repetitive.
No Map/Fast Travel: Lack of a map and fast travel between checkpoints can make navigation a hassle, forcing backtracking.
Performance: Occasional frame drops/lag could disrupt timing-dependent situations. 

Overall Verdict

Salt and Sacrifice is considered a successful distillation of the 2D Soulslike formula, offering addictive gameplay and strong execution, especially in its boss fights, making it a worthy and highly recommended experience for genre fans despite its reliance on familiar mechanics and minor flaws! Salt and Sanctuary was a majestic game.
Yet this... thing... I never thought that I would see something worse than Dark Souls II and yet - here I am.

Firstly: Salt & Sanctuary had a great, mysterious atmosphere - it is gone now. There is nothing left, everything is fragmented, it feels like the developer had too many thoughts and didn't manage to achieve any of them.

Secondly: combat encounters. Most of the bosses spawn minions, which results in quite a few attacks being unavoidable. Why wear light armor then? In the first game it did matter at least. 
Maybe my memory is a bit blurry, yet most of the enemies in this game send you flying across space with every hit. I won't uncover this in full, because perhaps a similar thing was present in the first game. I don't remember, but I have no memory of such pain.

Thirdly: exploration, discovery. This point is mostly connected to the first one. Again: the first game rewarded your curiosity, gave items, etc. This - gamemaking-attempt - does not. There is just nothing to mention... everything that lies within chests or somewhere else is useless (despite flask upgrades), as most items are to be gained through crafting.
In metroidvanias some of the content is locked until you gain a certain key item (like a hook, for example). It is fine, but those heart-locked doors are complete ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ - they just feel off, like the developer - again - had no time to completely finish the game and decided to put bosses in random places for you to chase them all around the map to unlock something.

Bonus thing: in the first game covenants (or whatever they were called) always granted you consumables after resting (there was a bit of a grind to level up covenant, but it was fine for me). In this game, after you rest you spend resources to replenish your consumables. EVERY consumable - healing bottles too.
It is a bad practice and a game-design choice (though as for healing flasks - one may not worry, there are always enough resources for replenishing).

So... I don't think that this game could become better than the first one, as it would require complete rearrangement, as it is too fragmented. If you liked Salt and Sanctuary - don't disappoint yourself, don't play this thing.

Compared to Sanctuary, I wouldn't say that the problem with Sacrifice is the levels getting split up into zones you warp to, or that there's a focus on multiplayer (which is completely dead). I would say that the problem with Sacrifice is that you're doing the same thing for the entire game.

Sanctuary was about exploring a massive island, trying to find the princess you lost at the start and also trying to find and escape, and interacting with NPCs and defeating whatever enemies are along the way. Sacrifice is a game about hunting mages, which you do by finding a point to trigger the hunt, then chasing the mage across the level to its final arena where you can then actually beat it. This is what you do for the entirety of Sacrifice. You start hunts, you chase mages to their arenas, and then you have the final battle with them.

Every mage has the same process to it, as you can't actually kill a mage before it gets to its arena; no matter how much damage you deal it just triggers them warping to the next location. Similarly, at each location the mage will summon minor monsters before moving on, which it no longer does at the final arena. So every mage feels the same, and every fight with them feels the same, and only the handful normal bosses sprinkled throughout the levels really feel like proper, unique bosses.

Additionally only a very scant few normal enemies throughout the game actually drop any equipment items at all; most of them just drop the same few material items for crafting potions and ammo. So fighting normal enemies feels like a waste of time in this. The game is also inherently much more linear than Sanctuary was, with your few key items and runes being taken away in NG+ despite there really being no reason for the key items at least, and the story manages to be even less forthcoming than Sanctuary's was.

There's still some fun to be had with the combat and exploration but in the end it's just a very tedious game with too much repetition of the same process over and over.

Let me first start off by saying I loved Salt and Sanctuary and I’m a big fan of the souls-like genre. If you liked the first game and were thinking of picking up the second, please be advised that Salt and Sacrifice ain’t it. 

The two main downfalls of this game for me is its world and its gameplay loop. 

The world is not seamless like the first game but more of a hub based system with different biomes like Demons Souls. This however isn’t the gripe. My gripe is that the level design of the biomes are absolutely horrendous. Biomes feel like one big jumping puzzle platformer than an actual world. Falling damage is definitely a thing and you will feel it. Mobs are hyper aggressive which isn’t bad on its own but with the platforming aspect makes mobs act like a gatekeeper of sorts kicking you off the platform you are trying to jump on while you then fall to your death. This is extremely aggravating and feels like bad design rather than bad gameplay. Also the various traps around the levels have such razor thin timing windows that it makes exploration feel like a chore rather than rewarding.

My other gripe is the gameplay loop. Yes, being an inquisitor set off to complete hit contracts to assassinate heretic mages sounds cool in theory. But, in execution this falls flat. Instead of a custom mission, these mages just run around the biome until you corner them with a boss fight. This got really old after the 4th or 5th contract due to the bad level design in the first place. Traversing the map already feels bad, so adding a moving boss who summons minions to the horrible level design makes this gameplay loop really frustrating.

I’m still going to try and 100% this garbage because I’m a psycho. If you’re a fan of the souls like genre there are better games to spend your money on. Get the original Salt and Sanctuary if you haven’t already as that game is great and doesn’t have the same issues this game does. If you must buy this game as there are no options left. Get it on sale.

中规中矩，说不上好，差其实也没差到哪去，目前评分低很大一部分原因是首发爆雷，然后很多人打了低评分之后没有再次修改，评分个人给到8.2-8.4分。
大致说下问题吧，首先其一，材料不写用途也不写来源，有些东西获得了不知道用来干嘛，需要用到一些材料也不知道要从哪获得。
其二，很多基础功能开局不告诉你，比如说武器技能，你想要使用武器技能的话是必须点出神圣或者咒文符节（这个在武器详情页切换到武器技能介绍会显示符文等级要求），但我认为这个设定其实某种程度上是反直觉的，因为符节加的是奥术伤害，如果不了解肯定会认为是法术类的东西，而和武器技能肯定想不到一块去，就比如说玩艾尔登法环你拿个大剑想玩狮子斩，结果发现必须要点智力才能用技能一样荒诞。
其三，地图设计过于复杂，板块联系很弱，捷径很多时候无法起到作用，真该加个小地图说实话，能打mod就打个mod吧。
其四，战斗方面，血瓶机制很恶心人，不同于大部分游戏血瓶是直接恢复大管血量的，他是喝了之后缓慢回血，就会导致你血多了喝药浪费，血少了喝药来不及的情况出现。（不过抛开这点，他的战斗其实我还挺喜欢的，能给到个8.8左右，他的轮椅武器非常强力，削弱了战斗机制缺陷的影响）
其五，后期地图探索非常糟糕，他这个游戏大部分BOSS打完会变成精英怪刷新在地图上，然后因为BOSS体型过大只会刷新在空旷地区，但是并没有那么多地方让他们刷新，就会导致好几个BOSS刷新在同一个地方，出现很严重的堆怪问题，在后期BOSS数值很高的情况下，BOSS随手一击能打你半管血，更别说BOSS还会召唤小怪（其实小怪比BOSS难打，实话），然后一人给你来一下直接送回复活点。
只能说能玩，但很难受，虽然想想一代也很多恶心点但总体还是好玩的，二代真没想到会改成这样

[u]Game got updated so I am updating this: This game is not bad but also not good. It is in desperate need of minor changes to core mechanics.[/u]

This game is fairly different from the first one. Story is nothing to write home about and aside from the repetetive grind for ressources propably the worst part. Addition of Multiplayer (many other reviews say it has problems) and amazing boss-design is the best part.

+ As mentioned leveldesign is good, areas connect to and fro via shortcuts or metroidvania items. The game also is not one interconnected world like the first game and instead lets you use a portal to reach different areas via a runesystem. Those areas are fairly big thou and are made up of quite diverse areas in and of themselves.

+++ Enemies look amazing and the higher their tier is the more attackpatterns they have (trashmobs > ranged > challenging variants > minibosses > proper bosses / mages), there is monster infighting which I like a lot. Had 4 mages duke it out and I was the victor at the end, really nice.

You guys should cut down on the hazecursed enemies those are spawning endlessly all around you during magehunts and will attack you and the mage. Or make them NOT spawn near ledges it makes some jumping puzzles impossible as they will attack you as you are approaching and will just knock you to your death over and over.

+ The mages are quite divers and are mostly fun to fight, but...

- The magefights... oh boy. The bad part is that someone had the foolish idea that there should be bosses that run away from you like in monsterhunter, but it doesnt work in this game. Monsterhunter has amazingly choreographed combat and interactible traps and the like, this game has nothing like that.

I had it happen that mages ran away into areas I had not yet unlocked which is dumb, there literally was a boss and a whole section between me and the mage. One way to go around this problem by the player is to explore first and clear as much as you can before farming, but a patch would still be nice.

- To top it off you have to redo magefights, with them fleeing and all that, over and over for crafting and upgrade material. You will get a pointer to the mage and a heartbeat as a signal but this doesnt work in freeroam straight away. No problem during a quest to hunt one, need an item to unlock those for a map thou > generates new quests every day. But have fun getting one that has the tier of items you need...

My character has reached level 100 and I got enough upgrades so that I can do the fights really fast most of the time, but mindless grinding shouldnt be needed to enjoy yourself (I upgraded and tried lots of weapons and this also artificially raises your playtime a lot.

If you look up the weapons then the whole magic of finding out what you can get from defeating the mages is gone... Come on, make us hunt them once and give a simple way to fight them again after or something else to get materials.

- The mages can summon creatures at any time during the fights and those guys can be as dangerous as a miniboss. This can be very frustrating because there is NO cooldown or limit, they just spam those guys out and some even buff the mage... Had a Mage [spoiler]Neuromancer (sweet name btw)[/spoiler] summon a floaty boy which gave him invincibility (remember those eyballs from the first game, those) and he summoned a second one while he was running away, the first caught up and made it very hard to keep figthing the actual target.

The mages also have [b]a lot[/b] of attacks that are AoE and will hit the whole arena and they are also able to spam these [spoiler]Bibliomancer[/spoiler], get hit by the first shot of a machinegunbarrage and you might eat the whole thing depending on poise. Fix this via cooldowns and add more melee > which would let the player actually parry them (you cant [b]sadface[/b]). CORRECTION, you can parry, but it will not happen much. Most mages dont attack melee enough to break their stamina! Bad design.

- Juggling and interrupting of your character is real and it ruins the experience when it happens, same with being flung off into all kinds of direction, especially off of things... Having a fight against a windmage on small platforms combined with incredible "throwing" ability of windmagic is no fun and the summons can do it as well. Making the damage lower each time you get juggled is ok as a fix, but the spam needs to stop as well. [b]Cooldowns![/b]

At least blocking works but the are NO shields in the game and only vanguard weaponclass gets the privilege of blocking 100%, hope you have high stamina for that thou.

+/- The devs have been really sadistic with some of the placements of traps, ranged enemies and destructable groundsurfaces. Very hard to see activators. Be aware!

By the way parrying works differently than the first game, you perfect block now. Need to do it multiple times in a short timeframe for the break of the enemy-stance to happen!

+ The whole armorsystem and damagetypes have been changed. This is a weird one, they removed slash, piercing and blunt damage (its all physical now) but added a lot of other damagetypes instead that are mixed. This adds the need to have some alternate damagetypes at hand for some fights. The mix of damagetypes is also amazing! I like the possibilties!

Did some testing some bows say poison but dont poison and one has a spore status effect which works. Is this a bug? The frostbow works well for applying statusdamage? I can be poisoned with multiple types at the same time thou, even stacking poison??

I did some testing and only runearts can poison on a weapon that has poison as damagetype, weird and confusing for the players.

+ I have a bow that deals light+fire damage for example, this is really cool but I feel like there are physical variants missing that are viable for use, even higher tiers are lower damage (need to check after update, scaling changed). There are concoctions which can be used to add elementaldamage to your weapon and it seems to work with any mundane weapon.

+/- Something about regular weapons is that compared to the mageweapons, which you craft, have a weaponart while regular weapons cant. I have not seen one that has an ability. Missed chance, add the ability to find arts and perhaps even change scaling through them! Yes that would be like Elden Ring but that system is amazing and could be build upon! Please consider, also add more of those weapons. [b]Go wild![/b]

+ Heavy weapons interrupt properly now in the latest patch! Thanks devs.

+ Armor, poise does work how you expect it after patch, but there is a weird thing about light armor there is only some that have poise, is this intended?

Heavy armor has really good poise but is [b]very[/b] heavy... have fun spending points on encumbrance, you will likely mix and match or level your stats/use item effects.

- Before I forget, character creation... You cant see the stats of the classes and cant tell what your starting gift is... perfect design choice [b]>sarcasm[/b]. Fix that asap. You can guess from the name what you will likely get but that is bad design. Make it clear! Or the player can look it up


Long story short, the devs have been on holiday while the game was out on Epic, well I guess as there are no means over there to report bugs (funfact there is in game) to them or tell them grievances (no forum on Epic) that is how it had to go.

I am teasing, the whole situation feels like a joke. You had a year to tweak the game to make it fully enjoyable and messed it up. This game can be fixed with little effort (first Patch was ok so far, but still ways to go)

> Add cooldowns to things, make the materials (higherlevel magefights) easier to access and fix what seem to be bugs. Think about adding some more fun gamemechanics like interactibles on the map! I like the game. It has so much cool stuff, but the issues are just frustrating! Careful thumbs up for now!

Playing this fully coop, and already it's miles above other souls-like games when it comes to it.  Everything is shared, if the host loots a chest & gets a quest item/piece of loot, their joined player can loot the same thing.  Enemies drop full xp for each player, with loot rolled separately (because of a magic find stat), and all progression is shared as well, just awesome.

Took two seconds to figure out how to join each other and play, and it works flawlessly so far.

A nice variety of weapons, with the skill tree being fairly large, although most of the points are either +1 to a stat (upgrade-able to 5 I think), or the ability to use a tier of weapon/armour, so not super in-depth, but plenty to work towards.

Some of the enemies are a pain of chain attacks, with bosses having a lot of area of effect skills that will limit the playable spaces in the arena, but none have felt super overtuned so far, all quite doable (although I'm only a couple of hours in).

Definitely a fun game, especially for the price, and looking forward to playing more..

An absolutely amazing game — I was really pleasantly surprised, especially since it has received so much criticism.
I’ve also played Salt and Sanctuary. Many people say that Salt and Sanctuary is much better.
I disagree. Both games are excellent, in my opinion.

However, I’ve only played Salt and Sacrifice in co-op, and that’s where the game truly shines. My friend and I had an absolute blast — so much so that this was the best co-op experience of the year for me. I’d even dare to call it a co-op masterpiece.

That said, I can imagine the single-player experience being quite harsh and punishing. I’m not sure I’d want to play this in single-player.

We played the game in 2025, and looking at its update history, it’s clear that over time the game has received patches and quality-of-life improvements. It now feels technically solid and polished. There were a few small bugs, but nothing that got in the way.

I have no hesitation recommending this game — especially if you play it online with a friend.
However, it’s worth watching some tutorials and guides to really understand the game, especially the character progression. Once you get your character developed, the game isn’t particularly difficult. There are a few challenging platforming sections, but nothing overly hard.

All in all, a delightful co-op experience. Give it some time and enjoy the journey.

I can’t wait to see what Ska Studios comes up with next.

如果你喜欢盐与避难所，那就不要来碰盐与牺牲，，你见过追逐战是玩家满世界追BOSS的追逐战吗
 [h3] 盐与牺牲/盐与献祭[/h3]
前一段时间和恋人本地合作打通了盐1，打的死去活来，动不动散财好几万，路也找的很痛苦，但整体的体验是痛并快乐着的，优秀的地图设计，多样的武器和信仰法术与魔法（虽然盐1魔杖和法杖是两种武器让我感到挺抽象的），庞大的天赋树，大部分BOSS战都打的挺爽的，，，好了不再说了，感兴趣请移步盐与避难所。
于是作为官方续作，盐与牺牲，我们也隔了不到一天光速入了，结果第一天就给我看傻了，评论区说的游戏有追逐战指的是我们玩家去追BOSS啊？打个BOSS，劈两刀就换区，你得追BOSS的尾气一路跟踪，最后到作战区域再打BOSS，，就，这样的战斗从开头几乎一直打到了结尾，除了一些关键的道具门前可能会塞个正常BOSS，其他大多数时候你要做的就是满地图找要追逐的“法师”，因为你要做装备就得找法师刷材料。
本作我最满意的地方就是圣坛石核心这片区域，只有这片区域能让我梦回盐与避难所，没有法师，只有BOSS战和地图探索，可惜就那么大点。
顺带一提，本作所有的怪，包括法师，包括小怪，一个个攻击欲望高得离谱，攻击前摇也短，抗性还高，他们一巴掌能把你打飞下悬崖，你却完全打不了他们一点硬直，，和这些玩意比起来，本作为数不多的BOSS简直亲民的不行。
虽然本作让我只想通关不想再刷傻逼法师了，但本作相比避难所还是有舒服一点的地方的，远程武器现在能作为副手武器存在了，，地图设计虽然不如一代那么连贯，但迷路的概率稍微小了那么一点，只要你记得哪儿的门需要几颗法师之心，，大部分地图不完全探全也不影响主线通关，，更重要的是，本作支持在线合作了，，但有失有得，一代的开背包传送功能没有了，如果有跳跳乐苦手就只能靠自己练了。
说到在线，本作还缝了很多东西进去，比如摇人支援，你可以使用蜡烛请求野生猎人，啊不是，野生调查官支援，也可以成为邪恶的入侵者去杀害其他世界的调查官，，虽然基本摇不到这两个玩法的人就是了。
如果你实在游戏荒，又非常喜欢盐1，那我能说什么呢，找上一个愿意陪你赤石的朋友，接受法师之祸仪式吧，祝你好运。

最后吐槽一下最后一张图动不动就给我坠机到地图最底部真的纯恶心人来的，，越到后期越看不到玩家留言字条了，感觉大伙都玩累了懒得留言了。
虽然我通关了，但是很难给出好评，继承一代的没地图的缺点，然后迷一样的超绝击飞距离，这些小怪我认为我没资格打败他们，因为他们一巴掌能把我扇出十万八千里直接回到最初的起点，而我打他纹丝不动，还有淘气爱跑的法师boss，还有打败法师boss以后随机在地图各处刷新的法师boss，让你体验飞一般的感受，真是绝绝子。

7.0/10

[h1]Game had the potential to be great, but wasted all of it[/h1]

Funny enough the worst part isn't even the constant mage chases as many reviews imply - it's tedious and annoying, but not what breaks the game, as you will not really have to craft more than one or two items from a single mage set, except the one you want the armor from.
The real pain here is that the difficulty and combat are clearly designed for multiplayer in mind. Getting 2- or 3-shot constantly, unavoidable attacks all over the place (especially when above 50% equip load it becomes almost impossible to play), 2 mages in a single screen all the time, enemies camping every ledge available, knocking you down miles into the abyss, poise being effectively useless the way it's implemented, getting stunlocked all the time, and so on.
The great base of its predecessor is there, but the combat is just way too off to be fun.

Maybe coop this game actually is good and fun, but could not test it.

[b]Do not buy unless you wanna coop this game or are ready to bite through the pain from beginning to end![/b]

I love this game. Creating builds in relatively simple, but still satisfying. Combat is solid and the gear is fun to experiment with. Are there some questionable design decisions? Perhaps. Launching on EPIC? Not ideal. Are you going to to die from ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ enemy placements and boss attack spam? You bet. Is the game deserving of all the poor reviews? In my opinion, no. 

Edit: After obtaining all the achievements, I found that platforming as a whole become rather unpleasant. Every platform is just a little to high or a little to far. Falling through platforms is inconsistent. Wind gust placement is inconvenient, just for the sake of it. Wall jump timing is unforgiving and inconsistent. The grappling hook "prompt" has NO tolerance for timing. For me, platforming became the main cause of game play fatigue. I still recommend the game, but the platforming is tedious at best and rage inducing at worst.

This game excels at being what it is.... a hybrid soulslike/monster hunter 2D platformer. As long as you keep that in mind, you'll have a good time. Go into this expecting something like a straight 2D platforming soulslike ala Salt & Sanctuary, and you'll have a very bad time.

That said, the game IS an acquired taste. It'll take you about an hour to get your feet under you, more if you're new to the monster hunter genre. However, once it clicks, it clicks hard. Platforming is smooth, enemies are varied, and areas feature loads of explorability and short cuts to open. The Mage Hunt mechanic is satisfying.

This isn't a game for everyone. If you like monster hunter games, give a try. If you love soulslikes, give it a fair shake. 

All in all I give it an 7.5 out of 10. Worth the price of admission, especially on sale.

I was very excited about this game as I am a huge fan of the first one. They changed so many things that worked really well in the 1st game, and made them just completely ass in this one.

The UI is just trash now. The inventory screen got better as I played, but the shop screens(most notably the blacksmith) is the worst UI. All the gear is under one tab. Luckily it bunches together sets, but having to scroll through  rows of stuff is just terrible. Things should have been separated better to make it easier to read and compare.

Upgrading weapons is now a confusing mess. Instead of a Class 1 weapon with a Ranking of 2, the upgraded version of the same gear just has a different symbol on it. No numbers telling you if that symbol is a high ranking symbol, just an arbitrary symbol that you now have too compare to another arbitrary symbol. Is it higher than what you own? Who knows! There's so many upgrades that its almost impossible to keep track of what symbol means 1 and which means 9. You kept it super simple in the first game, why change it?

You now have a camp you have to go back to if you want to do anything. This is where all your shops are, and the only place you can level up and set skill points. So the flow of what made the first game fun is shot. I now have to stop my run and leave the map to level up, and then go back and start over. 

Yup starting over. Now there's only 2-3 spawn points in a map that you can teleport to. You have no control over that. You can teleport from all of them but can only go back to a few. Given that you now have to leave to level up or upgrade gear it becomes a slow aggravating process to move forward in this game the further you get.

Range combat is now limited to X amount of ammo, and when you run out you have to sit there holding down a button while you craft more. That crafting is an all or nothing thing also. You don't slowly gain ammo overtime, you have to wait for the process to finish before you get any ammo at all. 

Mage hunts were a bad idea. I'm sorry, but I don't want to chase a mini boss through the level slowly picking away at it's health while also fighting the bad guys from the level that re-spawn when you start a Mage hunt. Then we get to where we're going to start the real fight, after which the hunt ends and the level enemies you just killed re-spawn again. Oh also, while you are hunting a mage boss another one could just be in the map at the same time, and now you gotta deal with that one and it's minions at the same time.

It honestly all boils down to the fact that there was a wonderfully beautiful game that they made, and instead of following that up by upgrading a few systems they decided to go a fully different direction. It ended up hurting the game.
